contents: REVIEW ARTICLEAnticoagulant prophylaxis to prevent asymptomatic deep veinthrombosis in hospitalized medical patients: a systematicreview and meta-analysisN. S. LLOYD, J. D. DOUKETIS, I. MOINUDDIN, W. LIM and M. A. CROWTHERDepartment of Medicine, McMaster University, and St Josephs Healthcare, Hamilton, ON, CanadaTo cite this article: Lloyd NS, Douketis JD, Moinuddin I, Lim W, Crowther MA. Anticoagulant prophylaxis to prevent asymptomatic deep vein

thrombosis in hospitalized medical patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Thromb Haemost 2008; 6: 405-14.Summary. Background: The effect of anticoagulant prophy-laxis on the prevention of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) shouldinclude an investigation of both clinical and subclinical DVT.We conducted a systematic review to determine whetheranticoagulant prophylaxis reduces the risk of asymptomaticDVT compared to no prophylaxis in at-risk hospitalizedmedical patients. Methods: MEDLINE, EMBASE, and theCochrane Library were searched through March 2007 forrandomized trials of anticoagulant prophylaxis for the preven-tion of asymptomatic DVT, assessed by venogram or ultra-sound. We assessed four outcomes: all asymptomatic DVT,asymptomatic proximal DVT, major bleeding and mortality.Random effects meta-analyses were performed and results wereexpressed using relative risk (RR) and 95% confidence intervals(95% CIs). Results: Four trials including 5516 patients wereeligible. Our pooled analysis demonstrated that compared toplacebo, anticoagulant prophylaxis was associated with asignificantly lower risk of any asymptomatic DVT (RR 0.51;95% CI 0.39-0.67) and asymptomatic proximal DVT(RR0.45;95% CI 0.31-0.65). Anticoagulant prophylaxis was associatedwith a significantly increased risk of major bleeding comparedto placebo (RR 2.00; 95% CI 1.05-3.79). There was nosignificant difference in the pooled estimate for all-causemortality. Anticoagulant prophylaxis conferred an absoluterisk reduction of any DVT and proximal DVT of 2.6% and1.8%, respectively, and was associated with a 0.5% absoluterisk increase in major bleeding. Conclusions: Anticoagulantprophylaxis is effective in preventing asymptomatic DVT inat-risk hospitalized medical patients but is associated with anincreased bleeding risk. The therapeutic benefits of anti-coagulant prophylaxis appear to outweigh the risks ofbleeding.
Keywords: deep vein thrombosis, medical patient, meta-analysis, systematic review, thromboprophylaxis.Introduction

Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) develops in 10-40% of at-riskhospitalized medical patients who do not receive anticoagulantprophylaxis [1,2]. Approximately 70-80% of such thrombi areclinically silent or asymptomatic and are detected when medicalpatients undergo objective screening for DVT [3,4]. Althoughthe clinical importance of asymptomatic DVT has beenquestioned, especially when used to assess the efficacy ofanticoagulant prophylaxis [5,6], it may be relevant in hospital-ized medical patients for two reasons. First, medical patientswho are recumbent may not develop the typical clinicalmanifestations of DVT, such as leg swelling, redness or pain,which occur in ambulatory patients [7,8]. Thus, when a clinicalprediction rule for DVT incorporating typical signs andsymptoms of DVT was applied to hospitalized patients, 10%(5/50) of patients with a low clinical probability for DVTactually had proximal DVT [9], whereas in the outpatientsetting proximal DVT was found in only 3% of patients with alow suspicion for DVT [10]. Secondly, many hospitalizedmedical patients are unable to reliably express symptoms ofDVT because of co-morbidities associated with a decreasedlevel of consciousness or impaired cognition. Based on theseconsiderations, the initial clinical manifestation of DVT inhospitalized medical patients may be life-threatening or fatalpulmonary embolism, as the typical premonitory symptoms ofleg pain and swelling may be absent.The American College of Chest Physicians Guidelines onAntithrombotic Therapy gives a Grade 1A recommendationfor the use of anticoagulant prophylaxis in at-risk medicalpatientsforthepreventionofDVT[1].Despitethisguideline, DVT prophylaxis is underused, with practiseaudits indicating that only 16-33% of at-risk medicalpatients receive prophylaxis [11,12]. It is not clear whyanticoagulant prophylaxis to prevent DVT is not broadlyimplemented in medical patients, as it is in at-risk surgicalpatients [13-15].

Correspondence: James D. Douketis, St Josephs Healthcare, Room F-544, 50 Charlton Ave East, Hamilton, ON L8N 4A6, Received 24 August 2007, accepted 26 October 2007In a meta-analysis that assessed the efficacy of anticoagulantprophylaxis to prevent clinically overt (or symptomatic) DVTin hospitalized medical patients [3], prophylaxis appeared toconfer a reduction in symptomatic DVT; however, this findingwas not statistically significant. Previous meta-analyses assess-ing the efficacy of anticoagulant prophylaxis to preventasymptomatic DVT are limited. One such study included trialsthat used unreliable diagnostic tests for DVT, such asimpedance plethysmography or fibrinogen leg scanning, toassess treatment efficacy [16]. The other study was carried outalmost 10 years ago and did not include more recent trials ofanticoagulant prophylaxis [4].To provide a more comprehensive and reliable assessment ofthe effects of anticoagulant prophylaxis to prevent DVT in at-risk hospitalized medical patients, we performed a systematicreview and meta-analysis focusing on studies that assessedanticoagulant prophylaxis to prevent asymptomatic DVT.Methods

Study objectivesThe primary objective was to address the following question: inhospitalized medical patients, does anticoagulant prophylaxisreduce the risk of asymptomatic DVT compared to noprophylaxis? The secondary objectives were to assess the effectsof anticoagulant prophylaxis on major bleeding and mortality.We adhered to the Quality of Reporting of Meta-analyses ofrandomized trials (QUOROM) statement in the reporting ofthis meta-analysis [17].Literature search strategyWe systematically searched the MEDLINE (1950-March2007), EMBASE (1980-2007, week 11), and the CochraneLibrary (2007, first quarter) databases using the medical subjectheadings venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, throm-boembolism and the text words deep vein thrombosis. Theseterms were combined with terms for drugs used for thrombo-prophylaxis (e.g. heparin, low-molecular-weight heparin).These terms were then combined with terms for the studydesign of interest (i.e. randomized controlled trial, clinicaltrial). The United States National Institute of Health registryof clinical trials was searched for ongoing trials, and referencelists of included studies and relevant review articles were handsearched. Content experts were contacted to identify un-published trials. There were no language restrictions forthe literature search.

Eligibility criteriaEach potentially eligible study was reviewed independently bytwo authors (N. S. Lloyd and I. Moinuddin) to assesssuitability. Studies were included if they satisfied all of thefollowing criteria: (i) randomized controlled trial (RCT)comparing anticoagulant prophylaxis vs. no prophylaxis inhospitalized medical patients (studies assessing mechanicalprophylaxis, such as compression stockings, were not consid-ered); (ii) the RCT assessed the prevention of asymptomaticDVT based on screening all patients with venography orvenous ultrasound. Additional outcomes of interest were theproportion of proximal DVTs, all-cause mortality, and majorbleeding. Studies were excluded if they used methods to screenfor DVT that are unreliable, such as impedance plethysmog-raphy or fibrinogen leg scanning [18], or if patients received aco-administered antithrombotic drug, such as aspirin, inaddition to an anticoagulant.Outcomes

We assessed the effects of anticoagulant prophylaxis on thefollowing outcomes: (i) any asymptomatic DVT; (ii) asymp-tomatic proximal DVT; (iii) major bleeding defined as bleedingrequiring transfusion of two or more units of packed red bloodcells, involving a critical site (e.g. retroperitoneal), or that wasfatal; and (iv) all-cause mortality. The primary outcome wasincidence of asymptomatic DVT (any, proximal) at the end ofthe treatment period. The secondary outcomes, major bleedingand all-cause mortality, were assessed at the end of the studyperiod. For each outcome, the summary risk was expressed as arelative risk (RR) and corresponding 95% confidence interval(CI), such that in the pooled analyses estimates <1.0 favor theanticoagulant group and estimates >1.0 favor the controlgroup.Data abstractionTwo reviewers (N. S. Lloyd and I. Moinuddin) used standard-ized forms to abstract the data independently and in duplicate.In the event of disagreement, a third reviewer (J. D. Douketis)was available to resolve discrepancies. A j-statistic wascalculated to assess reliability in the observed agreement. Thereviewers were not blinded to the journal name, author, orstudy outcomes. Because the incidence of DVT varies with riskfactors and co-morbidities, these factors were documentedfrom each of the primary studies.Study quality assessmentStudy quality was assessed using the validated five-point Jadadscale, which assesses appropriateness of randomization andblinding as well as whether the study gave an adequate reportof withdrawals [19]. Quality assessment was performed toevaluate the extent to which the study design, methods, andanalysis minimized the potential for bias.Statistical analysisThe results of the randomized trials were pooled usingRevMan version 4.2.10 for Windows (Cochrane Collabora-tion, Oxford, UK) to estimate the effect of anticoagulants onthe study outcomes. We decided a priori to use the more 2007 International Society on Thrombosis and Haemostasis

conservative random-effects model to estimate a treatmenteffect.In an attempt to reduce bias, we decided that the pooledanalysis would be conducted and presented according to anintention-to-treat analysis, which we defined as an analysis ofthe trial participants in the groups to which they wererandomized, regardless of which treatment they actuallyreceived and regardless of any other protocol irregularities.Further, all participants were included in the analysis, regard-less of whether their outcomes were actually collected [20].Potential heterogeneity across studies was assessed with theI2 statistic in RevMan. Provided there was no valid reason notto quantitatively analyze the data, such as statistical heteroge-neity that could not be explained, we made an a priori decisionto pool the results of the included trials in order to determinewhether there was an overall protective effect of anticoagulantadministration in hospitalized medical patients.ResultsLiterature searchOf the 910 articles identified, 889 were excluded after review oftitleandabstractand21studieswereretrievedforfurtherreview.Seventeentrialswerethenexcludedandfourtrialswereincludedin this meta-analysis [21-24]. A flow diagram of the identifica-tion of these trials is presented in Fig. 1. One of the trials waspublished in two parts, with the authors describing the studymethodology in a previous publication [21,25]. One study thatmet the eligibility criteria [26] was excluded because thepopulation included only acute stroke patients, who are ahigh-risk subgroup not comparable to typical hospitalizedgeneral medical patients. Most studies were excluded becausethey did not assess DVT prevention, hospitalized medicalpatients were not studied, there was not a placebo or noprophylaxis treatment arm, asymptomatic DVT was not

assessed, or the study was not an RCT. Seventeen studies werenot assessable based on title and abstract screening alone andfull reports were retrieved [27-42]. Nine of the seventeen fullreports retrieved were subsequently excluded because they usedfibrinogen scanning for DVT assessment [31,33-39,42]. Threestudies [27-29] were excluded because only symptomatic, andnot asymptomatic, DVT was assessed, one was a review article[40], and two were excluded because the patient population [30]or the treatment [32] did not meet the eligibility criteria. Onelargeunpublishedstudy,inwhichallmedicalpatientsweregivenenoxaparin for 10-14 days prior to randomization to activetreatment or control, was identified [41,43]. While the authorsreported a statistically significant reduction in venous throm-boembolism (VTE) in those patients receiving extended lowmolecular weight heparin (LWMH) therapy, the study wasexcluded because of the possible influence the treatment periodprior to randomization might have had on the overall studyresults. Agreement on study selection was perfect (j = 1.00).The inter-rater agreement on the data abstracted was excellent,with a weighted j-statistic of 0.93 (95% CI 0.87-0.99).Study characteristics

Thecharacteristicsandoutcomesofthefourincludedstudiesareoutlined in Tables 1 and 2. Three trials compared LMWH toplacebo [21,22,24] and one trial compared fondaparinux toplacebo [23]. The trial by Samama et al. [22] was a three-armRCT comparing two doses of enoxaparin (40 mg, 20 mg) withplacebo. Because the 20 mg dose is not widely used in clinicalpractise, we confined our comparison to the 40 mg dose withplacebo. The duration of treatment in the trial by Leizoroviczet al.[21]was14 days,andsimilarlythetreatmentperiodwas6-14 days in the trials by Cohen et al. [23] and Samama et al. [22].Only the trial by Fraisse et al. [24] had a longer treatment periodof22 days,butthemeandurationoftreatmentwas11 days.Fortwo of the trials, the total duration of follow-up was approx-imately 1 month [23,24]. The other two trials followed patientsfor three months or more [21,22]. It should be noted, however,that the follow-up for the primary outcome generally occurrednear the end of the treatment period. For instance, asymptom-atic DVT was assessed in the trial by Samama et al. [22] up today 14 and by Cohen et al. [23] up to day 15. Fraisse et al. [24]assessed patients for asymptomatic DVT within the treatmentperiod while Leizorovicz et al. [21] conducted their primaryefficacy assessment at day 21. Secondary outcomes weregenerally assessed at the end of the study period, but exceptionsto this are noted in Table 2.All trials were funded by the pharmaceutical companies thatmanufactured the drug under investigation and in two of thereports [21,23] the authors disclosed conflicts of interest in thatthey acted as consultants in addition to their investigator roles.Study qualityUsing the validated five-point Jadad scale to assess studyquality [19], there was perfect agreement between reviewersPotentially relevant studies identified and screened for retrieval (n = 910)

Studies excluded after full article  screening with inclusion criteria (n = 17) Fibrinogen leg scan: 8 Assessed symptomatic DVT only: 3 Heparinoid prophylaxis began w loading  dose of anti-factor-Xa: 2 Review paper: 1 Study population: 2  Patients received DVT prophylaxis prior to randomization: 1 Studies excluded after title and abstractscreening using inclusion criteria(n = 889)RCTs included in the systematic review and meta-analysis (n = 4) Studies retrieved for more detailed evaluation (n = 21)  2007 International Society on Thrombosis and Haemostasis(j = 1.00) that the overall methodological quality of thestudies was very good, with quality scores ranging betweenfour and five. All four studies were reported as randomizeddouble-blindtrialsandtheauthorsprovidedadequatedescriptions of withdrawals and reported appropriate meth-ods of double-blinding. However, three studies did not reportthe method used to generate the sequence of randomization[21,22,24].Study outcomesAny (proximal or distal) DVT The overall incidences ofDVT in patients who received prophylaxis and who did notreceive prophylaxis were 2.7% (74/2761) and 5.3% (146/2755),respectively. The pooled analysis (5516 patients) for any DVTis presented in Fig. 2. All four trials reported significantreductions in the risk of asymptomatic DVT in patientsrandomized to anticoagulant prophylaxis compared to placebo[21-24]. Anticoagulant prophylaxis was associated with asignificantly lower rate of any asymptomatic DVT thanplacebo (RR 0.51; 95% CI 0.39-0.67; p < 0.00001). The testfor heterogeneity of study results was not significant (I2 = 0%;p = 0.77).

Proximal DVT The incidences of proximal DVT in patientswho received prophylaxis and who did not receive prophylaxiswere 1.5% (40/2761) and 3.3% (90/2755), respectively. Thepooled analysis (5516 patients) for proximal DVT is presentedin Fig. 3. Anticoagulant prophylaxis was associated with asignificantly lower rate of proximal DVT than placebo (RR0.45;95%CI0.31-0.65;p < 0.0001).Thetestforheterogeneity of study results was not significant (I2 = 0%;p = 0.83).Major bleeding The incidences of major bleeding in patientswhoreceivedprophylaxisandwhodidnotreceiveprophylaxiswere1.0%(28/2761)and0.5%(14/2755),respectively. While there was a trend in all trials forplacebo patients to experience fewer major bleeding episodescompared to patients receiving anticoagulant prophylaxis,none of the individual trials reported statistically significantdifferences in major bleeding between groups. As shown inthe pooled analysis in Fig. 4, patients in the placebo grouphad a significantly lower risk of major bleeding thanpatients receiving anticoagulant prophylaxis (RR 2.00; 95%CI 1.05-3.79; p = 0.03). The test for heterogeneity of studyresults was not significant (I2 = 0%; p = 0.87). With theexception of one study [24], where the investigators alsoused a risk/benefit judgment in their definition of majorbleeding, the studies used similar definitions for majorbleeding.All-cause mortality The incidences of all-cause mortality inpatients who received prophylaxis and who did not receiveprophylaxis were 6.2% (170/2761) and 6.8% (186/2755),respectively. None of the trials reported significant differencesbetween groups in all-cause mortality. As shown in the pooledanalysis in Fig. 5, there was no significant difference in all-cause mortality in patients receiving anticoagulant prophylaxisor placebo (RR 0.89; 95% CI 0.70-1.14; p = 0.36). The test

The main difference between trialsin the assessment of this outcome was the time point ofassessment, which ranged from 21 days to 90 days.

DiscussionThe principal finding from this study is that in hospitalizedmedical patients who are at risk for developing DVT,anticoagulant prophylaxis confers a 49% reduction in the riskfor any (proximal or distal) asymptomatic DVT and a 55%reduction in the risk for asymptomatic proximal DVT. Thisfinding is consistent with the therapeutic efficacy observedwhen anticoagulant prophylaxis is administered in other at-riskhospitalized patients who have undergone major orthopedic orgeneral surgery [1] or who have suffered an ischemic stroke [44].Our finding is likely to be valid because it was based on studiesin which DVT assessment was based on reliable and objectivetesting (venography or venous ultrasound) and was evaluatedby independent adjudication committees that were blinded topatients treatment allocation.Anticoagulant prophylaxis conferred an almost 2-foldincreased risk of major bleeding compared to placebo.However, the trade-off between DVTs prevented and major 2007 International Society on Thrombosis and Haemostasis

bleeds incurred by anticoagulant prophylaxis favors the use ofprophylaxis. Thus, anticoagulant prophylaxis conferred anabsolute risk reduction of any DVT and proximal DVT of2.6% and 1.8%, respectively. However, anticoagulant prophy-laxis was associated with a 0.5% absolute risk increase in majorbleeding.Our finding that anticoagulant prophylaxis had no apparenteffect on all-cause mortality is likely due to almost all deathsbeing unrelated to the use (fatal bleeding) or non-use (fatalpulmonary embolism) of anticoagulant prophylaxis. The highoverall mortality (6.5%) of the population studied likely reflectsthe burden of disease in hospitalized medical patients.An important issue that should be addressed is theclinical significance of asymptomatic DVT as an outcomemeasure to assess the efficacy of anticoagulant prophylaxis.The significance of such clinically silent DVTs has beenquestioned, with the inference being that most of thesethrombi are destined to resolve spontaneously, do not causeembolic complications, and therefore do not merit imple-mentation of preventive interventions. There are several linesof reasoning, however, to support the clinical significance ofasymptomatic DVT in hospitalized medical patients. First,studies involving hospitalized patients who have undergonemajor orthopedic surgery (hip or knee replacement) dem-onstrate that the reduction of asymptomatic DVTs withanticoagulant prophylaxis correlates with a reduction insymptomatic events, thereby suggesting that, in fact, aconsiderable number of previously asymptomatic thrombiare destined to cause symptomatic DVT and pulmonaryembolism [45]. Secondly, a previous meta-analysis showedthat anticoagulant prophylaxis confers a 53% reduction inthe risk for symptomatic DVT in at-risk medical patients(RR 0.47; 95% CI 0.22-1.00) [3], which is consistent withthe 49% relative risk reduction for asymptomatic DVTobserved in the present study. Thirdly, 37% of asymptom-atic DVTs in medical patients involve the proximal veins[21-24], which most clinicians would consider clinicallyimportant and would treat. Finally, a population-basedcohort study found that among 516 patients who developedsymptomatic VTE in association with prior hospitalization,

67% of these events occurred during the first month afterdischarge from hospital, thereby suggesting that manythrombi that are clinically silent during hospitalization aredestined to become clinically overt [46].There are limitations of our study that should be addressed.First, one study relied on venous ultrasound to screen for DVT,which does not reliably detect calf DVT [21]. Although theprevalence of DVT would be under-represented in this study,thereby affecting estimates of overall prevalence of DVT, thisshould not affect our assessment of efficacy of anticoagulantprophylaxis, which was based on within-study comparisons oftreatment efficacy. Further, this does not impact on ourestimates of the rate of proximal DVT. Secondly, there were nohead-to-head comparisons of anticoagulants to assess relativeefficacy and safety, and our conclusions pertain to thethromboprophylactic effects of anticoagulants as a drug class.Thirdly, there was no standardized definition of bleeding acrossstudies, as this has only recently been proposed for medicalpatients [47]. However, this should not affect within-studycomparisons of safety of anticoagulants for DVT prophylaxis.The results of this meta-analysis have implications forclinical practice. First, our findings provide additional evidencethat anticoagulant prophylaxis reduces the risk of DVT.Secondly, our findings indicate that without prophylaxis, theincidence of DVT is considerable, as 5.3% of untreated patientswill develop any (proximal or distal) DVT and 3.3% of suchpatients will develop proximal DVT. Taken together, ourfindings support the need to implement recommendations fromconsensus guideline groups and other authorities that antico-agulant prophylaxis should be considered in at-risk hospital-ized medical patients. Additional research is needed to identifypatient groups in whom the therapeutic benefits from antico-agulant prophylaxis are greatest and to identify barriers (andmethods to overcome these barriers) to optimize the imple-mentation of anticoagulant prophylaxis.In summary, anticoagulant prophylaxis is effective inpreventing asymptomatic DVT in at-risk hospitalized medicalpatients, but is associated with an increased bleeding risk. Thetherapeutic benefits of anticoagulant prophylaxis appear tooutweigh the risks of bleeding.
